---

## DISCOVERY DATA

พบครั้งแรก:

- Episode

พบล่าสุด:

- Episode


---

## IMPORTANT CHARACTERS

บุคคลสำคัญ:

- ชื่อ
- บทบาท
- ความเกี่ยวข้อง


---

## RELATED FACTIONS

กลุ่มที่เกี่ยวข้อง:

- Pirates
- Marines
- Kingdoms
- Organizations


---

## SUB LOCATIONS

สถานที่ย่อย:

- อาคาร
- เขตพื้นที่
- จุดสำคัญ


---

## HISTORY

ประวัติสำคัญ:

บันทึกเหตุการณ์ที่เปลี่ยนแปลงสถานที่


---

## CURRENT EVENTS

เหตุการณ์ปัจจุบัน:

สิ่งที่กำลังเกิดขึ้น


---

## NOTES

ข้อมูลเพิ่มเติม


---

# 3. WORLD STATE TRACKING

สถานที่สามารถเปลี่ยนแปลงตามเวลา

ตัวอย่าง:

- เมืองถูกโจมตี
- รัฐบาลเปลี่ยน
- ประชากรลดลง
- เศรษฐกิจเปลี่ยน
- กลุ่มใหม่เข้าควบคุม


ทุกการเปลี่ยนแปลงต้องมีเหตุผลจากเหตุการณ์ในโลก


---

# 4. UPDATE RULES

การแก้ไขข้อมูลสถานที่ต้องเกิดจาก:

- เหตุการณ์ใน Episode
- การกระทำของผู้เล่น
- การกระทำของ NPC
- เหตุการณ์โลก


ห้าม:

- เปลี่ยนข้อมูลเพื่อช่วยผู้เล่น
- เปลี่ยนข้อมูลเพื่อบังคับเนื้อเรื่อง
- เพิ่มข้อมูลโดยไม่มีเหตุผล


---

# 5. RELATED DOCUMENTS

- SYSTEM.md
- RULES.md
- GAME_MASTER.md
- WORLD_DATABASE.md
- SAVE_FORMAT.md


---

# DOCUMENT STATUS

Document Name

LOCATION_DATABASE.md


Version

1.0


Engine Version

ASTER ENGINE v1.x


Status

Production


Authority

World Database


Last Updated

TBD


---

# END OF DOCUMENT

# =====================================================
# FILE : 01_WORLD\NAVIGATION.md
# =====================================================

# NAVIGATION

---

# Purpose

`NAVIGATION.md` defines the navigation simulation system of PROJECT ASTER ENGINE.

Navigation is more than movement.

It is the process of determining routes, surviving environmental hazards, managing uncertainty, and successfully reaching a destination.

Navigation is a core gameplay system affecting exploration, trade, military operations, piracy, and world simulation.

---

# Scope

This document defines:

- Navigation Principles
- Route Selection
- Navigation Equipment
- Magnetic Navigation
- Travel Accuracy
- Route Deviation
- Navigation Failure
- Exploration

This document does not define:

- Ship Statistics
- Weather Generation
- Individual Sea Regions
- Combat
- Sea Creatures

Those systems reference this document.

---

# Core Principle

Navigation is never guaranteed.

Every voyage carries uncertainty.

Knowledge reduces risk.

Preparation increases survival.

Poor navigation creates meaningful consequences.

---

# Navigation Requirements

Successful navigation requires:

- A seaworthy vessel.
- A destination.
- Navigation equipment.
- Crew cooperation.
- Environmental awareness.

Missing one or more requirements increases navigation difficulty.

---

# Navigation Methods

Possible navigation methods include:

- Visual Navigation
- Coastal Navigation
- Celestial Navigation
- Magnetic Navigation
- Local Knowledge
- Recorded Sea Routes
- Specialized Navigation Devices

Different regions may require different methods.

---

# Magnetic Navigation

Within the Grand Line,

standard compasses are unreliable.

Navigation depends on magnetic synchronization.

Specialized equipment is required.

Navigation equipment determines route reliability.

---

# Route Selection

Every voyage follows a selected route.

Possible route types include:

- Safe Route
- Trade Route
- Military Route
- Exploration Route
- Smuggling Route
- Emergency Route

Different routes carry different risks and rewards.

---

# Route Accuracy

Navigation accuracy depends on:

- Crew Skill
- Navigation Equipment
- Weather
- Sea Conditions
- Visibility
- Available Information

Reduced accuracy increases uncertainty.

---

# Route Deviation

Ships may deviate from their intended route.

Possible causes include:

- Storms
- Ocean Currents
- Navigation Errors
- Mechanical Failure
- Sea King Encounters
- Human Error

Deviation creates new situations rather than automatic failure.

---

# Exploration

Unexplored routes contain uncertainty.

Possible discoveries include:

- Unknown Islands
- Hidden Harbors
- Dangerous Waters
- Valuable Resources
- Rare Creatures
- Ancient Ruins

Exploration should always involve meaningful risk.

---

# Navigation Failure

Navigation failure does not necessarily destroy a voyage.

Possible consequences include:

- Delayed Arrival
- Lost Course
- Resource Consumption
- Unexpected Encounters
- Ship Damage
- Emergency Landing
- Crew Fatigue

Failure creates new gameplay opportunities.

---

# Information Gathering

Successful navigation benefits from information.

Useful information includes:

- Weather Reports
- Local Rumors
- Merchant Charts
- Marine Intelligence
- Previous Expeditions
- Historical Records

Knowledge improves decision making.

---

# Environmental Interaction

Navigation is affected by:

- Weather
- Wind
- Visibility
- Ocean Currents
- Sea State
- Magnetic Disturbance

Navigation systems must always consider current environmental conditions.

---

# Long-Distance Voyages

Long voyages require continuous navigation.

Conditions may change during travel.

The AI Game Master should periodically reevaluate navigation conditions throughout the journey.

---

# AI Game Master Responsibilities

The AI must:

- Apply realistic navigation challenges.
- Respect environmental conditions.
- Simulate uncertainty.
- Reward preparation.
- Prevent impossible travel.

The AI must never:

- Guarantee successful navigation.
- Ignore weather influence.
- Ignore magnetic anomalies.
- Teleport ships between destinations.

---

# Integration

Parent Documents:

- WORLD.md
- TIMELINE.md
- SEAS.md
- GRAND_LINE.md
- CALM_BELT.md
- WEATHER.md

Related Documents:

- SHIPS.md
- WORLD_EVENTS.md

Integrated Systems:

- DICE.md
- COMBAT.md
- REPUTATION.md
- SAVE_FORMAT.md

---

# Design Principles

Navigation is decision making.

Every route carries opportunity.

Every route carries danger.

Preparation reduces uncertainty.

Exploration rewards courage.

The sea never guarantees safe passage.

---

# Source of Truth

`NAVIGATION.md`

is the authoritative specification governing navigation, route selection, voyage accuracy, and travel uncertainty throughout PROJECT ASTER ENGINE.

All future systems involving ships, exploration, sea travel, and environmental movement must inherit the rules defined in this document.

# =====================================================
# FILE : 01_WORLD\NEWS_SYSTEM.md
# =====================================================

# NEWS_SYSTEM

---

# Purpose

`NEWS_SYSTEM.md` defines how information spreads throughout PROJECT ASTER ENGINE.

Information is not shared instantly.

Knowledge travels through realistic communication channels.

Every individual, organization, and nation only knows what they have reasonably learned.

The News System preserves information realism while allowing the world to react naturally to significant events.

---

# Scope

This document defines:

- Information Propagation
- News Sources
- Rumors
- Official Reports
- Public Awareness
- News Reliability
- Information Delay
- Information Persistence

This document does not define:

- Reputation
- Relationships
- World Events
- NPC Memory

Those systems reference this document.

---

# Core Principle

Information has a source.

Information has a path.

Information requires time.

Not everyone knows everything.

Knowledge must spread naturally.

---

# Information Sources

News may originate from multiple sources.

Examples include:

- Eyewitnesses
- Survivors
- Travelers
- Merchants
- Newspapers
- Marine Reports
- Government Announcements
- Den Den Mushi
- Criminal Networks
- Revolutionary Networks

Each source possesses different reliability.

---

# Information Categories

The Engine recognizes several categories of information.

Examples include:

- Local News
- Regional News
- Global News
- Military Reports
- Criminal Activity
- Political Events
- Natural Disasters
- Economic Changes
- Scientific Discoveries

Each category spreads differently.

---

# Information Propagation

News spreads gradually.

Factors affecting propagation include:

- Distance
- Transportation
- Communication methods
- Political censorship
- Public interest
- Reliability of witnesses

Information should never become globally known immediately without justification.

---

# Information Delay

Every piece of information requires time to spread.

Possible delays include:

- Minutes
- Hours
- Days
- Weeks

The delay depends on:

- Event significance
- Communication infrastructure
- Regional accessibility

Timeline consistency must always be maintained.

---

# Rumors

Rumors are unofficial information.

Rumors may be:

- Accurate
- Partially accurate
- Misunderstood
- Exaggerated
- Completely false

Rumors influence public perception regardless of their accuracy.

---

# Official Reports

Official reports originate from recognized authorities.

Examples include:

- Marine Headquarters
- Kingdom Governments
- Local Authorities
- World Government

Official reports are generally more reliable,

but may still contain intentional misinformation.

---

# Newspapers

Newspapers distribute important public information.

Possible contents include:

- Major battles
- Political developments
- Pirate activity
- Bounty updates
- Natural disasters
- Extraordinary discoveries

Publication requires time.

Distribution requires transportation.

---

# Public Awareness

Different populations possess different levels of awareness.

Examples:

A remote village may know nothing about a recent war.

Marine Headquarters may possess classified intelligence.

Pirates may possess underground information before governments.

Knowledge distribution is never perfectly uniform.

---

# Classified Information

Some information remains restricted.

Examples include:

- Military operations
- Intelligence reports
- Secret research
- Hidden facilities
- Political negotiations

Restricted information should only become public through realistic disclosure.

---

# Information Accuracy

Information quality varies.

Possible outcomes include:

- Verified
- Reliable
- Unconfirmed
- Conflicting
- False

Characters should react according to what they believe,

not necessarily objective reality.

---

# Information Persistence

Information becomes part of history.

Historical records may later be referenced by:

- Governments
- Scholars
- Journalists
- Criminal Organizations
- Historians

Information should not disappear without reason.

---

# News Influence

Information influences:

- Reputation
- Public Opinion
- Trade
- Politics
- Crime
- Military Operations
- Diplomatic Relations

The spread of information may generate additional World Events.

---

# AI Game Master Responsibilities

The AI must:

- Spread information realistically.
- Respect communication delays.
- Preserve uncertainty.
- Separate rumor from fact.
- Maintain historical consistency.

The AI must never:

- Give universal knowledge.
- Reveal secret information without cause.
- Ignore communication limitations.
- Instantly inform every NPC.

---

# Integration

Parent Documents:

- WORLD.md
- TIMELINE.md
- ISLAND_RULES.md

Related Documents:

- WEATHER.md
- WORLD_EVENTS.md

Integrated Systems:

- REPUTATION.md
- RELATIONSHIP.md
- FACTIONS.md
- NPCS.md
- SAVE_FORMAT.md

---

# Design Principles

Knowledge has value.

Information creates influence.

Influence changes behavior.

Behavior changes history.

The world reacts to what people know,

not merely to what actually happened.

---

# Source of Truth

`NEWS_SYSTEM.md`

is the authoritative specification governing information propagation throughout PROJECT ASTER ENGINE.

All future systems involving communication, rumors, intelligence, newspapers, public awareness, and historical records must inherit the rules defined in this document.

# =====================================================
# FILE : 01_WORLD\RED_LINE.md
# =====================================================

# RED_LINE

---

# Purpose

`RED_LINE.md` defines the simulation rules governing the Red Line.

The Red Line is the largest geological structure in the world.

It acts as a natural barrier separating the world's oceans while serving as a major political, strategic, and geographical landmark.

This document establishes how the Red Line functions within the world simulation.

---

# Scope

This document defines:

- Red Line Geography
- World Separation
- Surface Accessibility
- Strategic Importance
- Transportation Limitations
- Regional Influence

This document does not define:

- Individual Settlements
- Government Facilities
- Mariejois
- Fish-Man Island
- Reverse Mountain

These are defined in their respective documents.

---

# Core Principle

The Red Line is an impassable continental barrier.

It shapes the world's geography.

It influences politics, trade, military strategy, and exploration.

Its existence affects every sea in the world.

---

# Geological Identity

The Red Line is a massive landmass extending around the entire planet.

Unlike ordinary islands,

it forms a continuous geological barrier.

It permanently separates the world's oceans.

The Red Line cannot be bypassed through ordinary sailing.

---

# Global Separation

The Red Line divides the world's oceans into separate regions.

Travel between major sea regions is heavily restricted.

Movement across the Red Line requires specialized routes or methods defined elsewhere in the Engine.

---

# Accessibility

The majority of the Red Line is inaccessible.

Natural cliffs,

extreme elevation,

and harsh terrain prevent ordinary travel.

Only limited access points exist.

Those access points are defined in future documents.

---

# Strategic Importance

Control of locations connected to the Red Line provides significant strategic advantages.

Possible advantages include:

- Military deployment
- Trade regulation
- Transportation
- Intelligence gathering
- Political influence

The strategic value of Red Line locations should always be considered during world simulation.

---

# Political Influence

The Red Line affects global politics.

Nations,

organizations,

and military forces may compete to secure important routes connected to it.

Political tension surrounding the Red Line may change over time.

---

# Military Importance

The Red Line serves as a natural defensive structure.

Military organizations may use it to:

- Protect territory
- Control movement
- Monitor trade
- Restrict access
- Launch operations

Military presence should vary according to strategic importance.

---

# Economic Influence

Because the Red Line restricts movement,

it naturally influences:

- Trade routes
- Transportation costs
- Supply chains
- Resource distribution
- Regional markets

Changes affecting transportation routes may produce economic consequences.

---

# Environmental Influence

The Red Line influences surrounding environments.

Possible effects include:

- Wind patterns
- Ocean currents
- Migration routes
- Climate interaction

Detailed environmental mechanics are defined in WEATHER.md.

---

# Travel Restrictions

The Red Line cannot be crossed freely.

Players,

NPCs,

merchant vessels,

and military fleets are all subject to the same geographical limitations.

The AI Game Master must apply these restrictions consistently.

---

# World Simulation

The Red Line itself remains geographically stable.

However,

activity surrounding it continuously changes.

Possible ongoing developments include:

- Trade expansion
- Military movement
- Political negotiation
- Criminal operations
- Exploration

---

# AI Game Master Responsibilities

The AI must:

- Preserve geographical consistency.
- Respect movement restrictions.
- Simulate strategic importance.
- Apply identical rules to all characters.

The AI must never:

- Allow unrestricted crossing.
- Ignore geographical barriers.
- Treat the Red Line as ordinary terrain.
- Create unexplained travel shortcuts.

---

# Integration

Parent Documents:

- WORLD.md
- TIMELINE.md
- SEAS.md

Related Documents:

- GRAND_LINE.md
- CALM_BELT.md
- NAVIGATION.md
- WEATHER.md
- WORLD_EVENTS.md

Integrated Systems:

- DICE.md
- REPUTATION.md
- SAVE_FORMAT.md

---

# Design Principles

The Red Line defines the world's geography.

Geography shapes civilization.

Civilization shapes conflict.

Conflict shapes history.

---

# Source of Truth

`RED_LINE.md`

is the authoritative specification governing the Red Line and all systems directly influenced by its geographical existence.

Future systems involving transportation, politics, military strategy, and regional movement must inherit the rules defined in this document.

# =====================================================
# FILE : 01_WORLD\SEAS.md
# =====================================================

# SEAS

---

# Purpose

`SEAS.md` defines the global ocean structure of PROJECT ASTER ENGINE.

The sea is the primary environment of the world.

Nearly every journey, trade route, military operation, pirate activity, and exploration depends on the ocean.

This document defines how the oceans are divided and how they behave within the simulation.

---

# Scope

This document defines:

- Ocean Structure
- Sea Classification
- Regional Boundaries
- Ocean Navigation
- Ocean Simulation
- Cross-Sea Travel
- Sea Accessibility

This document does not define:

- Grand Line
- Red Line
- Calm Belt
- Weather
- Individual Islands

These are defined in their own documents.

---

# Core Principle

The ocean is the foundation of the world.

Every location belongs to a sea.

Every voyage occurs through connected oceans.

No island exists independently from the world's ocean system.

---

# World Ocean Structure

The world is divided into multiple major regions.

These regions are separated by natural barriers.

Each region has unique navigation conditions.

Each region develops independently while remaining connected through global events.

---

# Sea Classification

The Engine recognizes the following sea regions.

## North Blue

Stable ocean.

Moderate marine activity.

Moderate pirate activity.

Balanced trade routes.

---

## East Blue

Relatively peaceful.

Lower average pirate threat.

High civilian population.

Strong merchant activity.

---

## West Blue

Politically diverse.

Higher organized crime activity.

Moderate Marine presence.

---

## South Blue

Rich biodiversity.

Unpredictable regional conditions.

Frequent long-distance trade.

---

## Grand Line

Highly unpredictable.

Extreme environmental variation.

Advanced navigation required.

Defined in `GRAND_LINE.md`.

---

## Calm Belt

Extremely dangerous.

Minimal wind.

Minimal ocean current.

Dense Sea King population.

Defined in `CALM_BELT.md`.

---

# Ocean Boundaries

Every sea has physical boundaries.

Crossing into another region requires realistic travel.

The world never treats sea transitions as instant.

---

# Regional Identity

Each sea develops independently.

Possible differences include:

- Economy
- Crime
- Marine Activity
- Pirate Presence
- Technology
- Culture
- Trade
- Politics

No two seas are required to evolve identically.

---

# Ocean Simulation

Every sea continuously simulates:

- Trade
- Ship Movement
- Pirate Activity
- Marine Patrols
- Weather Influence
- Population Movement
- Economic Flow

Simulation continues regardless of player location.

---

# Navigation

Travel always follows navigable routes.

Ships cannot teleport.

Every voyage consumes:

- Time
- Resources
- Crew stamina
- Ship durability

Navigation rules are expanded in future documents.

---

# Sea Accessibility

Different regions have different accessibility.

Accessibility depends on:

- Geography
- Ocean Conditions
- Ship Capability
- Navigation Skill
- Government Restrictions
- Natural Hazards

The AI Game Master determines accessibility based on established world rules.

---

# Ocean Influence

The surrounding sea influences nearby islands.

Possible effects include:

- Economy
- Imports
- Exports
- Marine Presence
- Pirate Activity
- News Speed
- Travel Frequency
- Resource Availability

Island simulation must consider its surrounding sea.

---

# Cross-Regional Influence

Events occurring in one sea may eventually affect another.

Examples include:

- Economic shifts
- Pirate expansion
- Government campaigns
- Refugee movement
- Epidemics
- Trade disruption

The spread of influence requires time.

Immediate worldwide effects should be avoided unless justified.

---

# AI Game Master Responsibilities

The AI must:

- Maintain regional consistency.
- Simulate activity in every sea.
- Respect geographical boundaries.
- Continue world movement during player absence.
- Apply realistic travel limitations.

The AI must never:

- Ignore regional differences.
- Instantly move ships across oceans.
- Freeze inactive regions.
- Treat all seas identically.

---

# Integration

Parent Document:

- WORLD.md

Related Documents:

- TIMELINE.md
- GRAND_LINE.md
- RED_LINE.md
- CALM_BELT.md
- ISLAND_RULES.md
- WEATHER.md
- WORLD_EVENTS.md

Integrated Systems:

- DICE.md
- COMBAT.md
- REPUTATION.md
- SAVE_FORMAT.md

---

# Source of Truth

`SEAS.md`

is the authoritative specification for global ocean classification and regional sea simulation.

All sea-related documents inherit the rules defined here.

# =====================================================
# FILE : 01_WORLD\TIMELINE.md
# =====================================================

# TIMELINE

---

# Purpose

`TIMELINE.md` defines how time progresses throughout PROJECT ASTER ENGINE.

Time is one of the core simulation systems.

Every event, action, journey, recovery, investigation, construction, and world change must consume time.

The world continues to move regardless of player actions.

---

# Scope

This document defines:

- World Time
- Time Progression
- Time Scale
- Event Order
- Time Consumption
- Simultaneous Events
- Time Continuity
- AI Time Management

This document does not define:

- Weather
- Geography
- NPC Schedule
- World Events
- Island Systems

Those systems reference this document.

---

# Core Principle

Time never stops.

Time never rewinds.

Time never skips without consequence.

Every meaningful action consumes time.

The world advances continuously.

---

# Timeline Authority

Timeline is the single source of chronological truth.

Whenever multiple systems interact,

Timeline determines:

- Event order
- Recovery duration
- Travel duration
- Investigation timing
- News propagation
- Reputation changes
- World Event progression

No system may ignore time progression.

---

# World Clock

The world operates on a continuous clock.

Time always moves forward.

The Game Master is responsible for maintaining chronological consistency.

Every event receives a chronological position.

Earlier events cannot be affected by later events.

---

# Time Unit

The Engine recognizes the following units.

## Minute

Used for:

- Combat aftermath
- Medical treatment
- Short conversations
- Immediate actions

---

## Hour

Used for:

- Travel inside islands
- Repairs
- Investigation
- Patrol
- Commerce
- Rest

---

## Day

Used for:

- Sea travel
- Recovery
- Construction
- Training
- Island activities

---

## Week

Used for:

- Long voyages
- Political changes
- Economic fluctuation
- Organization movement

---

## Month

Used for:

- Territory expansion
- Government response
- Crew development
- World-scale consequences

---

# Action Time

Every meaningful action consumes time.

Examples

Combat

→ Minutes

Medical Treatment

→ Minutes to Days

Sea Travel

→ Hours to Weeks

Investigation

→ Hours to Days

Negotiation

→ Minutes to Hours

Ship Repair

→ Hours to Weeks

Training

→ Days to Months

The exact duration is determined by the relevant system.

---

# Simultaneous Events

Multiple events may occur simultaneously.

Examples

- Players are sailing.
- Marines launch an operation.
- Pirates attack another island.
- Storms develop.
- Merchants transport cargo.

The AI must continue simulating unrelated events.

The world never freezes while players are occupied.

---

# Time During Combat

Combat uses detailed time resolution.

Outside combat,

the world continues normally.

Large-scale events occurring elsewhere continue unless specifically interrupted.

---

# Time During Travel

Travel never skips time.

While traveling,

the following may occur:

- Weather changes
- Random encounters
- Marine patrols
- Pirate sightings
- Mechanical failures
- Sea King encounters
- World News updates

Travel is active simulation,

not fast travel.

---

# Downtime

Downtime represents periods without immediate conflict.

Possible downtime activities include:

- Training
- Reading
- Crafting
- Medical care
- Ship maintenance
- Crew interaction
- Commerce
- Information gathering

Downtime still advances world time.

---

# Time Dependency

Many systems depend on time.

Examples

Medical

Recovery requires time.

Relationship

Trust develops over time.

Reputation

Public opinion spreads over time.

Economy

Markets change over time.

World Events

Political situations evolve over time.

No system updates instantly without justification.

---

# Delayed Consequences

Consequences do not always occur immediately.

Examples

A Marine investigation may begin days later.

A newspaper may publish several days later.

A bounty adjustment may require weeks.

Political decisions may require months.

Delayed consequences improve world realism.

---

# Historical Record

Every completed event becomes history.

History cannot be erased.

Future systems may reference historical events.

Examples

Battles

Shipwrecks

Executions

Discoveries

Political agreements

Natural disasters

Crew formation

Historic events may influence future decisions.

---

# Timeline Consistency

The AI Game Master must prevent chronological contradictions.

Examples of invalid situations:

A character arriving before departure.

News appearing before an event occurred.

Recovery completing before treatment.

Construction finishing before work begins.

Cause must always precede effect.

---

# World Progression

Even if players remain inactive,

time continues.

Possible consequences include:

- Governments act.
- Pirates expand territory.
- Islands recover.
- Weather changes.
- Trade continues.
- Criminal activity increases.
- Marine patrols relocate.

Waiting is a decision with consequences.

---

# AI Game Master Responsibilities

The AI must:

- Track chronological order.
- Advance time consistently.
- Apply realistic durations.
- Continue world simulation.
- Synchronize simultaneous events.
- Preserve historical continuity.

The AI must never:

- Freeze the world.
- Ignore elapsed time.
- Skip consequences.
- Reverse chronology.
- Create impossible timelines.

---

# Integration

Referenced by:

- WORLD.md
- SEAS.md
- WEATHER.md
- WORLD_EVENTS.md
- NEWS_SYSTEM.md
- ISLAND_RULES.md

Integrated with:

- COMBAT.md
- DICE.md
- MEDICAL.md
- RELATIONSHIP.md
- REPUTATION.md
- SAVE_FORMAT.md

---

# Design Principles

Time is a resource.

Time creates opportunity.

Time creates consequences.

Time creates history.

Every action consumes time.

Every passing moment changes the world.

---

# Source of Truth

`TIMELINE.md`

is the authoritative specification for chronological progression inside PROJECT ASTER ENGINE.

All future systems that consume, manipulate, or reference time must follow the rules defined in this document.

# =====================================================
# FILE : 01_WORLD\WEATHER.md
# =====================================================

# WEATHER

---

# Purpose

`WEATHER.md` defines the global weather simulation system of PROJECT ASTER ENGINE.

Weather is a living world system that continuously changes over time.

It influences navigation, travel, combat, economy, exploration, communication, and world events.

Weather exists independently of player actions.

---

# Scope

This document defines:

- Weather Simulation
- Climate Zones
- Weather Progression
- Weather Intensity
- Environmental Effects
- Travel Influence
- Combat Influence
- World Interaction

This document does not define:

- Island Geography
- Navigation Mechanics
- Sea Kings
- Natural Disasters
- World Events

Those systems reference this document.

---

# Core Principle

Weather is dynamic.

Weather is persistent.

Weather is impartial.

Weather affects every character equally.

The world never maintains permanent weather unless explicitly defined.

---

# Weather Simulation

Weather continuously evolves throughout the world.

Every sea region maintains its own weather conditions.

Weather may change naturally over time.

Changes occur regardless of player location.

---

# Regional Independence

Weather is simulated independently for each region.

Possible regional differences include:

- Clear skies
- Heavy rain
- Thunderstorms
- Dense fog
- Snowfall
- Heat waves
- Strong winds
- Calm conditions

Neighboring regions are not required to share identical weather.

---

# Climate Zones

Every island belongs to a climate environment.

Possible climates include:

- Tropical
- Temperate
- Desert
- Arctic
- Volcanic
- Jungle
- Mountain
- Mixed Climate

Climate influences the probability of future weather.

Climate does not determine weather with certainty.

---

# Weather States

Possible weather conditions include:

- Clear
- Cloudy
- Rain
- Heavy Rain
- Storm
- Thunderstorm
- Fog
- Snow
- Blizzard
- Heat Wave
- Strong Wind
- Extreme Wind

Additional weather types may be introduced by future systems.

---

# Weather Intensity

Every weather condition possesses an intensity.

Suggested levels include:

- Mild
- Moderate
- Severe
- Extreme

Higher intensity produces greater environmental influence.

---

# Weather Duration

Weather is temporary.

Every weather state has a beginning,

a duration,

and an ending.

Weather should transition naturally whenever possible.

Abrupt changes require reasonable explanation.

---

# Environmental Effects

Weather may influence:

- Visibility
- Temperature
- Sea Conditions
- Wind Strength
- Ground Conditions
- Natural Hazards

Environmental effects should remain logically consistent.

---

# Travel Influence

Weather directly affects travel.

Possible consequences include:

- Reduced speed
- Delayed arrival
- Increased fuel consumption
- Sail efficiency changes
- Dangerous sea conditions
- Route adjustments

Travel systems must reference current weather conditions.

---

# Combat Influence

Weather may influence combat.

Examples include:

- Reduced visibility
- Limited ranged attacks
- Slippery terrain
- Strong winds
- Lightning hazards
- Extreme heat
- Extreme cold

Combat systems determine the exact mechanical effects.

---

# Economy Influence

Weather may affect local economies.

Examples include:

- Fishing productivity
- Agriculture
- Shipping
- Port activity
- Resource availability

Economic changes occur naturally over time.

---

# Population Behavior

Citizens respond realistically to weather.

Possible responses include:

- Seeking shelter
- Delayed travel
- Cancelled markets
- Increased indoor activity
- Emergency preparation

NPC behavior should reflect environmental conditions.

---

# Weather Progression

Weather changes gradually.

Typical progression may include:

Clear

↓

Cloudy

↓

Rain

↓

Storm

↓

Heavy Storm

↓

Rain

↓

Cloudy

↓

Clear

The AI Game Master should avoid unrealistic oscillation between extreme weather conditions.

---

# Extreme Weather

Extreme weather is uncommon.

Examples include:

- Hurricanes
- Massive Thunderstorms
- Blizzard Conditions
- Extreme Heat
- Oceanic Super Storms

Extreme weather may trigger World Events.

---

# Weather Memory

Weather leaves consequences.

Examples include:

- Flood damage
- Crop failure
- Port destruction
- Shipwrecks
- Landslides

Environmental recovery requires time.

---

# AI Game Master Responsibilities

The AI must:

- Simulate weather continuously.
- Maintain regional consistency.
- Respect climate influences.
- Apply weather equally.
- Preserve environmental continuity.

The AI must never:

- Ignore weather effects.
- Change weather without reason.
- Favor players through weather.
- Maintain static weather indefinitely.

---

# Integration

Parent Documents:

- WORLD.md
- TIMELINE.md
- SEAS.md
- ISLAND_RULES.md

Related Documents:

- NEWS_SYSTEM.md
- WORLD_EVENTS.md

Integrated Systems:

- COMBAT.md
- DICE.md
- MEDICAL.md
- SHIPS.md
- REPUTATION.md
- SAVE_FORMAT.md

---

# Design Principles

Weather creates uncertainty.

Uncertainty creates preparation.

Preparation creates meaningful decisions.

Weather should shape the world,

not merely decorate it.

---

# Source of Truth

`WEATHER.md`

is the authoritative specification governing weather simulation throughout PROJECT ASTER ENGINE.

All future systems involving climate, environmental conditions, travel, combat, and world simulation must inherit the rules defined in this document.

# =====================================================
# FILE : 01_WORLD\WORLD.md
# =====================================================

# WORLD

---

# Purpose

`WORLD.md` defines the fundamental simulation rules of the game world.

This document is the primary reference for how the world exists, changes, and responds to player actions.

It is **not** a lore document.

It is an Engine Specification used by the AI Game Master to consistently simulate a living world throughout the entire campaign.

All world-related systems must inherit the rules defined in this document.

---

# Scope

This document defines:

- World Structure
- World Simulation
- Reality Rules
- Time Progression
- Cause & Effect
- Persistent World Logic
- Player Interaction with the World
- AI World Simulation Responsibilities

This document does **not** define:

- Individual islands
- Geography details
- Weather systems
- Factions
- NPC generation
- Devil Fruits
- Ships

Those are defined in their respective documents.

---

# Core Philosophy

PROJECT ASTER ENGINE is designed as a **Narrative Simulation Engine**, not a traditional RPG.

The world exists independently of the players.

Players are not the center of the universe.

The world never waits for player actions.

Instead,

Players become one small part of an already living world.

---

# Parallel World Rule

PROJECT ASTER ENGINE takes place inside a

**ONE PIECE Parallel World**

The world follows every fundamental rule established by the ONE PIECE universe.

Including:

- Geography
- Ocean structure
- Grand Line
- Red Line
- Calm Belt
- Devil Fruits
- Haki
- Marines
- World Government
- Pirate System
- Navigation
- Sea Kings
- Den Den Mushi
- Technology Level
- World Economy

However,

All important story characters are original.

The narrative never depends on canon characters.

Canon events are not required to occur.

The Parallel World has its own history.

---

# Living World Principle

The world always exists.

It never pauses.

It never freezes.

It never waits.

Every location continues to operate regardless of player presence.

Examples:

- Shops continue trading.
- Marines continue patrolling.
- Pirates continue sailing.
- Islands continue developing.
- Governments continue making decisions.
- Criminals continue committing crimes.

If players ignore an event,

the event still progresses.

---

# Persistent World

Everything that happens inside the world becomes part of history.

The world permanently remembers:

- Battles
- Deaths
- Alliances
- Betrayals
- Crimes
- Discoveries
- Political changes
- Island destruction
- Major disasters

Nothing automatically resets.

---

# Cause and Effect

Every meaningful action creates consequences.

Consequences may be:

Immediate

Examples

- Injury
- Property damage
- Reputation changes

Delayed

Examples

- Marine investigation
- Revenge
- Economic impact
- Political instability

Long-term

Examples

- Government reforms
- New pirate crews
- Territorial conflicts
- Civil wars

The AI Game Master must always evaluate consequences based on realism.

---

# World Momentum

The world possesses its own momentum.

Events continue naturally without player involvement.

Examples

- Pirate crews continue traveling.
- Merchant ships continue transporting cargo.
- Weather changes naturally.
- Nations continue political decisions.
- Marine fleets relocate.
- Criminal organizations expand or collapse.

Player actions may redirect world momentum,

but never stop it entirely.

---

# Player Position

Players are inhabitants of the world.

They are never treated as chosen heroes.

No hidden advantages exist.

No invisible protection exists.

No destiny exists.

Players must earn influence through actions.

---

# No Plot Armor

The Engine never protects players for narrative reasons.

Players may:

- Lose battles
- Lose ships
- Lose crew members
- Become captured
- Become imprisoned
- Become permanently injured
- Die

NPCs follow the same rules.

---

# World Memory

The world remembers.

Information may spread through:

- Witnesses
- Newspapers
- Marine reports
- Rumors
- Survivors
- Den Den Mushi
- Investigation

Information is never instantly known by everyone.

Knowledge spreads over time.

---

# Information Limitation

Characters only know what they could realistically know.

The AI Game Master must separate:

Player Knowledge

from

Character Knowledge.

NPC decisions must never use information they could not have obtained.

---

# Dynamic World

The world continuously changes.

Possible changes include:

- Weather
- Economy
- Territory
- Governments
- Pirate influence
- Marine activity
- Population
- Trade
- Security
- Reputation

No location remains permanently static.

---

# Simulation Layers

The world operates on multiple layers simultaneously.

## Global Layer

Represents worldwide events.

Examples

- Government decisions
- Worldwide news
- Global conflicts

---

## Regional Layer

Represents sea-wide activity.

Examples

- Fleet movement
- Pirate influence
- Regional weather
- Trade routes

---

## Local Layer

Represents island-level simulation.

Examples

- Town economy
- Crime
- Citizens
- Shops
- Local conflicts

---

## Personal Layer

Represents individual characters.

Examples

- Relationships
- Reputation
- Injuries
- Goals
- Memories

---

# AI Game Master Responsibilities

The AI Game Master must:

- Simulate the world continuously.
- Never prioritize players unfairly.
- Apply rules consistently.
- Remember previous events.
- Preserve world continuity.
- Maintain realistic consequences.
- Resolve uncertainty using the Dice System.
- Respect all existing Engine documents.

The AI must never:

- Ignore previous events.
- Create plot armor.
- Force dramatic moments.
- Manipulate outcomes for entertainment.
- Break established world rules.

---

# Integration

This document serves as the parent specification for:

- TIMELINE.md
- SEAS.md
- GRAND_LINE.md
- RED_LINE.md
- CALM_BELT.md
- ISLAND_RULES.md
- WEATHER.md
- NEWS_SYSTEM.md
- WORLD_EVENTS.md

It also integrates with:

- SYSTEM.md
- RULES.md
- GAME_MASTER.md
- COMBAT.md
- DICE.md
- HAKI.md
- MEDICAL.md
- STATUS_EFFECTS.md
- RELATIONSHIP.md
- REPUTATION.md
- SAVE_FORMAT.md

---

# Design Principles

Every future world system must satisfy the following principles:

1. Consistency over spectacle.

2. Simulation over scripting.

3. Consequences over convenience.

4. Player agency over predetermined outcomes.

5. Persistence over reset.

6. Realism within the rules of the ONE PIECE universe.

7. Every action changes the world.

8. Every change becomes history.

---

# Source of Truth

`WORLD.md`

is the highest-level specification for all world simulation systems.

If any future world-related document conflicts with this file,

the rules defined in `WORLD.md` take precedence.

# =====================================================
# FILE : 01_WORLD\WORLD_EVENTS.md
# =====================================================

# WORLD_EVENTS

---

# Purpose

`WORLD_EVENTS.md` defines how world-scale events are generated, developed, propagated, resolved, and remembered throughout PROJECT ASTER ENGINE.

World Events are independent simulation processes.

They are not quests.

They are not scripted storylines.

They emerge naturally from the world's current state and continue regardless of player involvement.

---

# Scope

This document defines:

- World Event Generation
- Event Lifecycle
- Event Escalation
- Event Resolution
- Event Persistence
- Event Interaction
- Global Consequences

This document does not define:

- Individual island events
- NPC behavior
- Combat mechanics
- Reputation mechanics
- Faction mechanics

Those systems reference this document.

---

# Core Principle

Events are consequences.

The world continuously produces events.

Events influence the world.

Events influence other events.

History is created through accumulated events.

---

# World Event

A World Event is any meaningful occurrence capable of changing the state of the world.

Examples include:

- War
- Pirate invasion
- Government operation
- Economic crisis
- Disease outbreak
- Natural disaster
- Scientific discovery
- Revolutionary movement
- Assassination
- Trade disruption
- Major expedition

Events may occur at any scale.

---

# Event Sources

Events may originate from:

- Natural phenomena
- Governments
- Pirate crews
- Revolutionary organizations
- Criminal organizations
- Civilian activity
- Economic change
- Environmental change
- Previous events

Events should always have a believable origin.

---

# Event Lifecycle

Every World Event follows the same lifecycle.

Event Creation

↓

Event Development

↓

Event Escalation (optional)

↓

Event Resolution

↓

Historical Record

No event should appear or disappear without explanation.

---

# Event Development

Events evolve over time.

Possible developments include:

- Expansion
- Containment
- Negotiation
- Failure
- Escalation
- Fragmentation
- Recovery

Development depends on world conditions rather than narrative necessity.

---

# Event Escalation

Some events grow larger.

Examples include:

Minor Crime

↓

Organized Crime

↓

Regional Crisis

↓

National Emergency

↓

Global Incident

Escalation is not guaranteed.

The world determines whether escalation occurs.

---

# Event Resolution

Events may conclude through:

- Natural resolution
- Military intervention
- Political negotiation
- Economic recovery
- Scientific advancement
- Player action
- NPC action

Every resolution produces consequences.

---

# Event Persistence

Completed events remain part of world history.

Future systems may reference historical events.

Examples include:

- Memorials
- Political reforms
- Public opinion
- Territorial disputes
- Cultural change
- Economic recovery

Events are never erased.

---

# Event Interaction

Multiple events may influence one another.

Examples:

A hurricane destroys a port.

↓

Trade declines.

↓

Food shortages occur.

↓

Crime increases.

↓

Marine deployment expands.

↓

Pirate activity shifts.

↓

Political instability develops.

One event may generate multiple additional events.

---

# Event Priority

Events differ in significance.

Suggested priority levels include:

- Local
- Regional
- National
- Global

Priority affects:

- News coverage
- Government response
- Resource allocation
- Public awareness

Priority does not determine importance to individual players.

---

# Event Duration

Events consume time.

Possible durations include:

- Minutes
- Hours
- Days
- Weeks
- Months
- Years

Long-term events may continue throughout an entire campaign.

---

# Event Visibility

Not every event is publicly known.

Possible visibility states include:

- Secret
- Restricted
- Local Knowledge
- Regional Knowledge
- Global Knowledge

Visibility depends on the News System.

---

# Event Consequences

Every completed event should influence one or more systems.

Possible affected systems include:

- Reputation
- Relationships
- Economy
- Politics
- Security
- Population
- Trade
- Territory
- Weather
- Navigation

No significant event should leave the world unchanged.

---

# Event Chains

Events may trigger additional events.

Examples include:

Storm

↓

Shipwreck

↓

Missing Cargo

↓

Economic Loss

↓

Food Inflation

↓

Civil Unrest

↓

Revolution

↓

Government Response

↓

Military Deployment

↓

Political Reform

The AI Game Master should encourage logical event chains.

---

# Player Interaction

Players may:

- Prevent events.
- Accelerate events.
- Redirect events.
- Participate in events.
- Ignore events.

Ignoring an event is a meaningful decision.

The event continues unless realistically stopped.

---

# AI Game Master Responsibilities

The AI must:

- Continuously simulate world events.
- Preserve logical causality.
- Respect previous events.
- Apply realistic consequences.
- Maintain historical continuity.
- Prevent contradictory event timelines.

The AI must never:

- Create events solely for dramatic effect.
- Ignore completed events.
- Remove consequences without reason.
- Force players into events.
- Freeze world development.

---

# Integration

Parent Documents:

- WORLD.md
- TIMELINE.md
- WEATHER.md
- NEWS_SYSTEM.md
- ISLAND_RULES.md

Related Documents:

- SEAS.md
- GRAND_LINE.md
- RED_LINE.md
- CALM_BELT.md

Integrated Systems:

- REPUTATION.md
- RELATIONSHIP.md
- COMBAT.md
- MEDICAL.md
- FACTIONS.md
- NPCS.md
- SAVE_FORMAT.md

---

# Design Principles

The world creates events.

Events create consequences.

Consequences create history.

History shapes the future.

Players influence history,

but history does not exist solely because of players.

---

# Source of Truth

`WORLD_EVENTS.md`

is the authoritative specification governing the generation, progression, interaction, resolution, and historical persistence of world events throughout PROJECT ASTER ENGINE.

All future systems that create, modify, respond to, or record world events must inherit the rules defined in this document.

# =====================================================
# FILE : 02_PLAYER\AMBITIONS.md
# =====================================================

# AMBITION SYSTEM

---

# Purpose

The Ambition System defines the medium-term objectives that guide a character's current direction within the Narrative Simulation Engine.

Unlike Dreams, which represent a character's ultimate life aspiration, Ambitions represent concrete goals that can realistically be achieved over months or years.

Ambitions provide momentum.

Dreams provide purpose.

---

# Design Philosophy

Every character should always have something they are actively pursuing.

An Ambition answers the question:

"What is this character trying to accomplish right now?"

Ambitions give direction to daily decisions and naturally generate adventures.

---

# Dream vs Ambition

Dreams and Ambitions are separate systems.

Dream

- Long-term
- Philosophical
- Defines life purpose
- Rarely changes

Ambition

- Medium-term
- Practical
- Defines current objectives
- Changes throughout the campaign

Example

Dream

Become the world's greatest navigator.

Current Ambition

Acquire a ship capable of reaching the Grand Line.

After achieving that goal, a new Ambition naturally replaces it.

---

# Character Creation

Every playable character begins with one Primary Ambition.

The Ambition should support the character's Dream.

It should also fit:

- Background
- Occupation
- Current Situation
- Available Resources
- Personal Relationships

---

# Characteristics of a Good Ambition

A meaningful Ambition is:

- Achievable
- Specific
- Actionable
- Relevant
- Challenging

Examples:

Earn enough money to buy a ship.

Recruit a trustworthy crew.

Become a licensed doctor.

Open a restaurant.

Join the Marines.

Study under a famous shipwright.

Find a Devil Fruit.

Master Observation Haki.

Explore an uncharted island.

---

# Personal Motivation

Every Ambition should answer:

Why is this important now?

Understanding motivation helps both the player and Game Master create meaningful stories.

---

# Multiple Ambitions

Characters may possess several Ambitions.

However,

Only one should be considered the Primary Ambition at any given time.

Additional Ambitions remain secondary.

Too many simultaneous goals reduce narrative focus.

---

# Dynamic Nature

Ambitions naturally evolve.

Examples:

Buying a ship

↓

Finding a crew

↓

Crossing Reverse Mountain

↓

Exploring Paradise

↓

Entering the New World

Each completed Ambition leads naturally toward another.

---

# Failure

Failure does not permanently remove an Ambition.

Characters may:

Try again.

Find another method.

Delay their plans.

Abandon the goal.

Create a new objective.

Failure becomes part of the journey.

---

# Success

Completing an Ambition represents meaningful progress.

Possible rewards include:

New opportunities.

New allies.

Improved Reputation.

Professional recognition.

Personal confidence.

Access to new locations.

Narrative progression.

Completion should feel significant.

---

# Conflict

Ambitions may conflict with:

Dreams.

Relationships.

Crew Goals.

Faction Orders.

Political Events.

Moral Beliefs.

These conflicts create meaningful decisions.

---

# Shared Ambitions

Several characters may pursue the same Ambition.

Examples:

Building a crew.

Defeating a pirate fleet.

Protecting an island.

Opening a business.

Shared Ambitions strengthen cooperation while preserving individual Dreams.

---

# Hidden Ambitions

NPCs possess their own Ambitions.

These goals influence:

Daily behavior.

Long-term planning.

Political decisions.

Professional choices.

Personal relationships.

Players discover NPC ambitions through interaction.

---

# Abandoning an Ambition

Characters may voluntarily abandon an Ambition.

Reasons may include:

Failure.

Changing priorities.

New responsibilities.

Personal growth.

Loss.

Changing an Ambition should always have believable narrative justification.

---

# Relationship With Reputation

Persistent pursuit of an Ambition influences Reputation.

Examples:

A doctor known for treating the poor.

A merchant expanding across multiple islands.

A Marine hunting dangerous pirates.

A pirate challenging stronger opponents.

The world notices determination.

---

# Relationship With Character Growth

Ambitions encourage growth through:

Training.

Travel.

Experience.

Relationships.

Professional development.

Every completed Ambition changes the character in some way.

---

# Relationship With the Living World

The world does not wait.

While a character pursues an Ambition:

Enemies continue acting.

Governments continue changing.

Markets fluctuate.

Wars begin.

Rumors spread.

Characters must adapt their plans to an evolving world.

---

# Game Master Guidelines

The Game Master should use Ambitions to:

Create quests.

Introduce opportunities.

Generate conflicts.

Reward preparation.

Encourage roleplaying.

Develop long-term character arcs.

Ambitions should naturally guide stories rather than dictate them.

---

# Future Expansion

Future Engine modules may introduce:

Crew Ambitions.

Faction Ambitions.

National Ambitions.

Political Ambitions.

Economic Ambitions.

Every expansion must remain compatible with the Core Ambition System.

---

# Engine Principles

The Ambition System follows:

- Story Before System
- Narrative Simulation
- Hidden But Fair
- World Before Player
- No Plot Armor

Dreams define who a character wishes to become.

Ambitions define what they choose to do today.

---

# End of Ambition System

# =====================================================
# FILE : 02_PLAYER\ATTRIBUTES.md
# =====================================================

# ATTRIBUTE SYSTEM

---

# Purpose

The Attribute System defines the innate physical and mental capabilities of every character within the Narrative Simulation Engine.

Attributes represent natural potential rather than learned expertise.

They serve as the foundation for all character actions and interact with Skills, Equipment, Haki, Devil Fruits, Status Effects, and other gameplay systems.

Attributes rarely change over a character's lifetime.

Growth is primarily represented through Skills, Experience, and Narrative Development.

---

# Design Philosophy

Attributes describe what a character is naturally capable of.

Skills describe what a character has learned.

Equipment describes what a character possesses.

Power Systems describe extraordinary abilities.

These systems are intentionally separated.

---

# Core Attribute Model

Every playable character possesses six Core Attributes.

Each attribute represents a broad area of capability rather than a specific statistic.

The six Core Attributes are:

- Strength
- Agility
- Endurance
- Intelligence
- Awareness
- Presence

No additional base attributes exist.

---

# Strength

Strength represents raw physical power.

Strength influences:

- Lifting
- Grappling
- Climbing
- Breaking Objects
- Melee Force
- Physical Labor

Strength does not determine accuracy.

---

# Agility

Agility represents speed, coordination, precision, and balance.

Agility influences:

- Dodging
- Acrobatics
- Stealth Movement
- Balance
- Reflexes
- Fine Motor Control

Agility does not determine endurance.

---

# Endurance

Endurance represents physical resilience.

Endurance influences:

- Stamina
- Fatigue Resistance
- Injury Resistance
- Long-distance Travel
- Survival
- Physical Recovery

Endurance is not equivalent to health points.

---

# Intelligence

Intelligence represents reasoning and accumulated knowledge.

Intelligence influences:

- Investigation
- Learning
- Medicine
- Engineering
- Navigation Theory
- Tactical Planning

Intelligence does not represent wisdom or perception.

---

# Awareness

Awareness represents perception and environmental understanding.

Awareness influences:

- Observation
- Detecting Danger
- Tracking
- Reading Situations
- Searching
- Instinctive Reactions

Awareness supports but does not replace Observation Skills.

---

# Presence

Presence represents force of personality.

Presence influences:

- Leadership
- Persuasion
- Negotiation
- Intimidation
- Inspiration
- Social Confidence

Presence does not guarantee success in conversations.

NPC reactions always depend on context.

---

# Attribute Scale

All Core Attributes use the same rating scale.

| Rating | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Extremely Poor |
| 2 | Poor |
| 3 | Below Average |
| 4 | Average |
| 5 | Above Average |
| 6 | Excellent |
| 7 | Exceptional |
| 8 | Elite |
| 9 | Legendary |
| 10 | World-Class |

Ratings above 10 are not naturally attainable.

---

# Starting Limits

New player characters begin within the limits defined by the Character Creation System.

Characters cannot begin as legendary individuals.

Initial attribute distribution must remain balanced.

The exact allocation method is defined by the Character Creation rules.

---

# Attribute Checks

Whenever an action relies primarily on natural ability, the Game Master determines the most appropriate Attribute.

Examples:

Breaking a locked door

→ Strength

Balancing on a narrow mast during a storm

→ Agility

Marching for several days without rest

→ Endurance

Diagnosing an unfamiliar disease

→ Intelligence

Spotting an ambush before it begins

→ Awareness

Negotiating with a suspicious merchant

→ Presence

---

# Combined Checks

Many actions require both Attributes and Skills.

Examples:

Swordsmanship

→ Agility + Weapon Skill

Emergency Surgery

→ Intelligence + Medical Skill

Ocean Navigation

→ Intelligence + Navigation Skill

Tracking a Fugitive

→ Awareness + Survival Skill

Public Speech

→ Presence + Leadership Skill

The Game Master determines the most appropriate combination.

---

# Attribute Contest

When two characters oppose each other using natural ability, both sides make an appropriate Attribute Check.

Examples:

Arm Wrestling

Strength vs Strength

Chasing on Rooftops

Agility vs Agility

Staring Contest

Presence vs Presence

Holding Breath Underwater

Endurance vs Endurance

The higher successful result gains the advantage.

---

# Attribute Limitations

High Attributes do not guarantee success.

Environmental conditions, injuries, fatigue, fear, equipment, weather, and narrative circumstances may all influence outcomes.

The world remains the primary authority.

---

# Temporary Modifiers

Attributes are normally permanent.

Temporary effects may modify performance without permanently changing the Attribute.

Examples:

- Exhaustion
- Injury
- Poison
- Hunger
- Weather
- Motivation
- Fear

Temporary modifiers expire when their source is resolved.

---

# Permanent Changes

Permanent Attribute changes are extremely rare.

Possible causes include:

- Extraordinary Narrative Events
- Significant Physical Transformation
- Unique Devil Fruit Effects
- Engine-approved Story Milestones

Permanent changes require explicit Game Master approval.

---

# NPC Attributes

NPCs use the same Attribute framework as player characters.

Important NPCs follow the complete Attribute System.

Minor NPCs may use simplified Attribute profiles when appropriate.

---

# Hidden Information

NPC Attributes are hidden from players unless revealed through gameplay.

Players judge opponents through observation, not numerical values.

---

# Engine Principles

The Attribute System follows:

- Story Before System
- Narrative Simulation
- Hidden But Fair
- World Before Player
- No Plot Armor

Attributes define natural potential.

Choices determine how that potential shapes the world.

# =====================================================
# FILE : 02_PLAYER\BACKGROUNDS.md
# =====================================================

# BACKGROUND SYSTEM

---

# Purpose

The Background System defines a character's life before becoming an active participant in the campaign.

A Background explains who the character was before the story begins.

It establishes personal history, education, social standing, early experiences, relationships, opportunities, and worldview.

Backgrounds provide narrative context rather than predetermined destiny.

---

# Design Philosophy

Every individual has a past.

That past shapes:

- Knowledge
- Values
- Habits
- Relationships
- Opportunities
- Fears
- Strengths
- Weaknesses

Backgrounds explain where a character came from.

They do not determine where the character will go.

---

# Narrative Role

Backgrounds exist to answer fundamental questions.

Examples:

Who raised this character?

Where did they grow up?

How were they educated?

What major events shaped them?

Why did they leave home?

What do they believe?

Who remembers them?

The answers create narrative consistency throughout the campaign.

---

# Background Creation

Every playable character must possess one primary Background.

The Background is selected during Character Creation.

The chosen Background becomes a permanent part of the character's history.

---

# Background Components

Every Background contains the following elements.

- Origin
- Childhood
- Education
- Family
- Community
- Early Occupation
- Significant Event
- Starting Relationships
- Starting Reputation
- Personal Values

Each component contributes to the character's identity.

---

# Origin

Origin identifies where the character comes from.

Examples include:

- East Blue
- South Blue
- North Blue
- West Blue
- Paradise
- New World
- Sky Island
- Calm Belt Settlement
- Remote Island

Origin influences cultural familiarity but does not grant mechanical bonuses.

---

# Childhood

Childhood defines the character's early environment.

Examples include:

- Fishing Village
- Busy Port
- Noble Estate
- Marine Base
- Merchant Family
- Traveling Circus
- Mountain Settlement
- Shipyard
- Religious Community

Childhood shapes perspective rather than ability.

---

# Education

Education represents formal and informal learning before the campaign.

Possible sources include:

- Family
- Apprenticeship
- Marine Academy
- Private Tutor
- Self-Education
- Religious Institution
- Local School
- Practical Experience

Education supports believable Skill selection.

---

# Family

Family relationships provide narrative anchors.

Family may include:

- Parents
- Grandparents
- Siblings
- Adoptive Family
- Guardians
- Extended Relatives

Family members remain part of the living world unless otherwise established.

---

# Community

Every character belonged to a community before becoming an adventurer.

Communities influence:

- Reputation
- Customs
- Expectations
- Local Knowledge
- Personal Connections

Communities continue to evolve even after the character leaves.

---

# Early Occupation

Many characters worked before the campaign.

Examples include:

- Fisher
- Doctor
- Carpenter
- Hunter
- Merchant
- Sailor
- Guard
- Farmer
- Blacksmith

Occupation is determined separately within the Occupation System.

Background explains why that occupation was learned.

---

# Significant Event

Every Background contains one defining life event.

Examples:

- Natural disaster
- Pirate attack
- Marine rescue
- Family tragedy
- Great discovery
- Shipwreck
- Political conflict
- Disease outbreak

The event explains why the character's life changed.

---

# Values

Background influences personal values.

Examples:

- Justice
- Freedom
- Wealth
- Honor
- Knowledge
- Family
- Duty
- Adventure

Values influence roleplaying but never remove player agency.

---

# Starting Knowledge

Characters begin with knowledge appropriate to their Background.

Examples:

A fisherman understands local waters.

A doctor recognizes common diseases.

A merchant understands trade customs.

A navigator understands basic charts.

Background knowledge should always remain believable.

---

# Cultural Familiarity

Characters are naturally familiar with the culture in which they were raised.

This familiarity may include:

- Customs
- Dialects
- Food
- Festivals
- Superstitions
- Local Laws

Characters may misunderstand unfamiliar cultures.

---

# Starting Relationships

Background establishes existing relationships.

Examples:

Friends

Teachers

Employers

Family

Childhood Rivals

Former Shipmates

These individuals remain active members of the world.

---

# Starting Reputation

Most characters begin as ordinary people.

Some Backgrounds may provide:

Positive reputation

Negative reputation

Professional recognition

Family reputation

Local notoriety

Reputation never guarantees future treatment.

NPCs judge characters based on current behavior as well as reputation.

---

# Resources

Background may explain access to:

Tools

Books

Property

Family Business

Professional Contacts

Safe Locations

These resources belong to the story rather than the player.

The Game Master determines whether they remain accessible.

---

# Background Secrets

Some Backgrounds contain hidden information.

Examples:

Unknown heritage

Hidden inheritance

Forgotten promises

False identity

Government records

Secret organizations

Players may know or may not know these secrets depending upon the campaign.

The Game Master determines disclosure.

---

# Background Conflicts

A Background should create unresolved narrative opportunities.

Examples:

Missing family member

Debt

Broken friendship

Former mentor

Failed promise

Old enemy

These conflicts generate future stories.

---

# Changing Background

A character's Background never changes.

However,

Its meaning may change as new information is revealed.

Characters discover new truths about their own past throughout the campaign.

---

# Interaction With Other Systems

Background interacts with:

Character Creation

Occupation

Skills

Relationships

Reputation

Dreams

Ambitions

Lifestyle

NPC System

Faction System

World System

Every interaction must remain logically consistent.

---

# Game Master Guidelines

The Game Master should use Backgrounds to:

Introduce story hooks.

Create believable NPC connections.

Generate emotional investment.

Reward thoughtful character creation.

Expand the living world.

Background should never be ignored after Session 0.

---

# Engine Principles

The Background System follows:

Story Before System.

Narrative Simulation.

Hidden But Fair.

World Before Player.

No Plot Armor.

Every character begins the story with a past.

That past continues to shape the future long after the adventure begins.

---

# End of Background System

# =====================================================
# FILE : 02_PLAYER\CHARACTER.md
# =====================================================

# PLAYER CHARACTER

## Basic Information

Name:
โจเซ่ ไวท์แมน

Age:
18

Gender:
ชาย

Race:
มนุษย์

Origin Sea:
East Blue

Origin Island:
(ยังไม่ตั้งชื่อ)

Occupation:
อดีตแพทย์ทหารเรือ

Current Role:
แพทย์ประจำเรือ (เป้าหมาย)

Devil Fruit:
Tori Tori no Mi, Model: Phoenix

Status:
เริ่มต้นการเดินทาง

---

## Background

โจเซ่ ไวท์แมน เกิดบนเกาะเล็กแห่งหนึ่งใน East Blue
ซึ่งมีฐานทัพเรือตั้งอยู่

พ่อของเขาเป็นแพทย์ทหารเรือ
ส่วนแม่เสียชีวิตตั้งแต่เขายังเด็ก

หลังจากการสูญเสียแม่
พ่อของโจเซ่เริ่มเปลี่ยนไป

เขาไม่ได้เลี้ยงโจเซ่เหมือนลูกอีกต่อไป
แต่เริ่มมองว่าโจเซ่ต้องแข็งแกร่งเพื่อเอาชีวิตรอดในโลกที่โหดร้าย


เมื่ออายุ 6 ปี
โจเซ่ถูกบังคับให้กินผลปีศาจ

ผลปีศาจนั้นคือ:

Tori Tori no Mi, Model: Phoenix


หลังจากนั้นเขาถูกส่งไปฝึกกับ CP
เป็นเวลา 12 ปี

ตั้งแต่อายุ 6 ปี จนถึง 18 ปี


ชีวิตของโจเซ่เต็มไปด้วย:
- การฝึกต่อสู้
- การฝึก Rokushiki
- การฝึกแพทย์สนาม
- ภารกิจและการทดสอบ


เขาไม่ได้ต้องการเป็นอาวุธ
เขาไม่ได้ต้องการรับใช้ใคร

สิ่งที่โจเซ่ต้องการคือ:

"อิสระในการเลือกชีวิตของตัวเอง"

---

## Relationship: Father

Status:
Broken Relationship


Father:
อดีตแพทย์ทหารเรือ


โจเซ่และพ่อมีความสัมพันธ์ที่ไม่ดีต่อกัน

หลังจากแม่ของโจเซ่เสียชีวิต
พ่อของเขาเปลี่ยนไป

เขาเชื่อว่าการทำให้โจเซ่แข็งแกร่ง
คือวิธีเดียวที่จะทำให้ลูกชายรอดชีวิตในโลกใบนี้


แต่สำหรับโจเซ่:

การถูกบังคับให้กินผลปีศาจ
และถูกส่งไปฝึกกับ CP ตั้งแต่เด็ก

ไม่ใช่การปกป้อง

แต่มันคือการถูกพรากชีวิตวัยเด็กไป


ความรู้สึกของโจเซ่ต่อพ่อ:

- โกรธ
- ไม่ยอมรับสิ่งที่พ่อทำ
- ไม่ต้องการกลับไปใช้ชีวิตแบบเดิม
- ไม่ต้องการเป็นเครื่องมือของใคร


---

## Marine Base Incident

Age:
18 ปี


หลังจากจบการฝึกกับ CP
โจเซ่กลับมายังฐานทัพเรือใน East Blue


ในวันนั้นเกิดเหตุการณ์โจมตีโดยกลุ่มโจรสลัด


การต่อสู้เกิดขึ้นอย่างรุนแรง

ฐานทัพเรือถูกทำลาย
ทหารเสียชีวิตจำนวนมาก


โจเซ่ไม่ได้ถูกฆ่าโดยตรง

ระหว่างเหตุการณ์ เรือของฐานทัพถูกทำลาย
แรงระเบิดทำให้เขากระเด็นตกทะเล


เขารอดชีวิต
แต่ถูกกระแสน้ำพัดไปยังเกาะแห่งหนึ่งใน East Blue


เหตุการณ์นี้กลายเป็นจุดเริ่มต้นของชีวิตใหม่


จากอดีตทหาร

สู่ชายหนุ่มที่กำลังเลือกเส้นทางของตัวเอง

---

## Abilities


# Devil Fruit

## Tori Tori no Mi, Model: Phoenix

Type:
Mythical Zoan


โจเซ่ได้รับพลังผลปีศาจนี้ตั้งแต่เด็ก
จากการตัดสินใจของพ่อ


ความสามารถ:

- เปลี่ยนร่างเป็นฟีนิกซ์
- เพิ่มความสามารถทางร่างกาย
- ฟื้นฟูบาดแผล
- ใช้เปลวไฟฟื้นฟู
- เพิ่มความสามารถในการเอาตัวรอด


Combat Usage:

โจเซ่ไม่ได้ใช้พลังฟีนิกซ์เพื่อบุกอย่างเดียว

แต่ใช้เพื่อ:

- รับความเสียหายแทนผู้อื่น
- ยื้อเวลาในสนามรบ
- รักษาตัวเองและเพื่อนร่วมทีม


ข้อจำกัด:

- การฟื้นฟูใช้พลังงานจำนวนมาก
- ไม่สามารถรักษาทุกอาการได้
- ยิ่งบาดเจ็บหนัก ยิ่งใช้พลังมาก


---

# Rokushiki

โจเซ่ได้รับการฝึกจาก CP

สามารถใช้รูปแบบทั้งหกได้


## Soru

การเคลื่อนที่ด้วยความเร็วสูง


## Geppo

การเคลื่อนที่กลางอากาศ


## Tekkai

การเสริมความแข็งแกร่งของร่างกาย


## Kami-e

การควบคุมร่างกายเพื่อหลบการโจมตี


## Rankyaku

การโจมตีด้วยแรงเตะ


## Shigan

การโจมตีด้วยนิ้ว


---

# Haki


## Observation Haki

Status:
ใช้งานได้


เหมาะกับรูปแบบการต่อสู้ของโจเซ่

ใช้เพื่อ:
- อ่านการเคลื่อนไหว
- หาจังหวะโจมตี
- คาดการณ์อันตราย


---

## Armament Haki

Status:
ใช้งานได้


ใช้เพื่อ:
- ป้องกันการโจมตี
- เสริมการสวนกลับ
- ต่อสู้กับผู้ใช้พลังพิเศษ


---

## Conqueror's Haki

Status:
ยังไม่ตื่น


Potential:
มีโอกาสตื่นในอนาคต


---

# Fighting Style

## Phoenix Counter Doctor Style


รูปแบบการต่อสู้เฉพาะตัวของโจเซ่


แนวคิด:

"ไม่จำเป็นต้องโจมตีก่อน
แค่รอให้ศัตรูเปิดช่อง"


รูปแบบหลัก:

- ตั้งรับ
- อ่านคู่ต่อสู้
- ลดความเสียหาย
- สวนกลับ


จุดเด่น:

- ใจเย็นในสนามรบ
- วิเคราะห์ร่างกายคู่ต่อสู้
- ใช้ความรู้แพทย์ร่วมกับการต่อสู้


แม้โจเซ่จะเป็นคนใจร้อนในชีวิตประจำวัน

แต่เมื่อเข้าสู่การต่อสู้หรือการรักษา

เขาจะมีสมาธิและควบคุมตัวเองได้อย่างสมบูรณ์

---

# Personality


## General Personality

โจเซ่เป็นคนที่ภายนอกดูเหมือนไม่จริงจังกับชีวิต

เขาเป็นคน:

- ขี้เกียจ
- ชอบนอน
- ชอบอู้งาน
- ขี้บ่น
- ใจร้อน
- ติดบุหรี่
- ชอบดื่มเหล้า


เขาไม่ชอบชีวิตที่มีระเบียบและคำสั่งมากเกินไป
เพราะอดีตของเขาเต็มไปด้วยการถูกควบคุม


---

## Professional Personality

แม้ในชีวิตประจำวันจะดูเหมือนไม่เอาไหน

แต่เมื่อเข้าสู่สถานการณ์จริง:

### การรักษา

โจเซ่จะ:

- มีสมาธิสูง
- ใจเย็น
- รอบคอบ
- ตัดสินใจเร็ว
- ไม่ปล่อยให้อารมณ์เข้ามาแทรก


สำหรับเขา:

ชีวิตของคนไข้สำคัญกว่าความรู้สึกของตัวเอง


---

### การต่อสู้

เมื่อเข้าสู่สนามรบ

โจเซ่จะเปลี่ยนเป็นอีกคน

เขา:

- อ่านสถานการณ์
- คำนวณความเสี่ยง
- รอจังหวะสวนกลับ
- ไม่เสียการควบคุมง่าย ๆ


---

# Likes

- การเดินทาง
- อิสระ
- การค้นพบสถานที่ใหม่
- การช่วยเหลือผู้คน
- อาหารอร่อย
- เวลาพักผ่อน
- การนอน


---

# Dislikes

- การถูกควบคุม
- คำสั่งที่ไม่มีเหตุผล
- คนที่ใช้ชีวิตคนอื่นเป็นเครื่องมือ
- องค์กรที่มองคนเป็นสิ่งของ
- การถูกบังคับให้เลือกทางเดินชีวิต


---

# Habits

- สูบบุหรี่เวลาคิดมาก
- ดื่มเหล้าเวลาผ่อนคลาย
- ชอบหาข้ออ้างเพื่อไม่ทำงาน
- ชอบบ่นก่อนเริ่มทำอะไร


แต่เมื่อมีคนต้องการความช่วยเหลือ:

เขาจะเป็นคนแรกที่ลงมือ


---

# Strengths

- ความสามารถด้านการแพทย์สูง
- ใจเย็นในสถานการณ์วิกฤต
- มีประสบการณ์เกินวัย
- อ่านคู่ต่อสู้เก่ง
- ไม่ทอดทิ้งคนของตัวเอง
- มีความรับผิดชอบเมื่อถึงเวลาสำคัญ


---

# Weaknesses

- มีปัญหากับอำนาจและคำสั่ง
- ใจร้อนเรื่องส่วนตัว
- ไม่ชอบขอความช่วยเหลือ
- ใช้ชีวิตประมาท
- มีนิสัยทำลายสุขภาพตัวเอง
- แบกรับปัญหาคนเดียว


---

# Core Character

ภายนอก:

"หมอขี้เกียจติดเหล้า ที่ดูเหมือนไม่สนใจอะไร"


ภายใน:

"เด็กคนหนึ่งที่ถูกบังคับให้โตเร็วเกินไป
และกำลังพยายามค้นหาชีวิตที่เป็นของตัวเอง"


---

# Dream

ความฝันของโจเซ่:

เดินทางรอบโลกอย่างอิสระ

เขาต้องการเป็นหมอที่สามารถช่วยเหลือผู้คนได้ทุกที่

ไม่ขึ้นกับรัฐบาล
ไม่ขึ้นกับองค์กร
ไม่ขึ้นกับคำสั่งของใคร


เขาไม่ได้ต้องการเป็นคนที่แข็งแกร่งที่สุด

เขาแค่อยากมีสิทธิ์เลือกว่าตัวเองจะใช้ชีวิตอย่างไร

---

# Starting Status


## Current Affiliation

Affiliation:
None


Previous Affiliation:
Marine / CP Training


Current Role:
ไม่มีสังกัด


---

# Starting Location

Sea:
East Blue


Location:
Unknown Island


Situation:

โจเซ่ถูกพัดขึ้นฝั่งหลังจากเหตุการณ์ทำลายฐานทัพเรือ

ไม่มีเรือ
ไม่มีลูกเรือ
ไม่มีชื่อเสียง

เขาเริ่มต้นใหม่ในฐานะชายธรรมดาคนหนึ่งที่มีเพียงความสามารถของตัวเอง


---

# Reputation

World Reputation:
Unknown


Marine Reputation:
อดีตบุคลากรที่หายสาบสูญ


Pirate Reputation:
ไม่มี


---

# Bounty

Current Bounty:

0 Berry


Reason:

ยังไม่มีค่าหัว
เนื่องจากยังไม่มีการกระทำที่ทำให้รัฐบาลโลกสนใจ


---

# Equipment


## Medical Equipment

- กระเป๋าแพทย์ส่วนตัว
- อุปกรณ์ปฐมพยาบาลพื้นฐาน
- ยารักษาเบื้องต้น


## Personal Items

- เสื้อผ้าที่ติดตัวมา
- บุหรี่
- อุปกรณ์จุดไฟ
- ของใช้ส่วนตัวเล็กน้อย


## Weapons

ไม่มีอาวุธประจำตัว


เหตุผล:

โจเซ่พึ่งพา:
- Rokushiki
- Haki
- พลังผลปีศาจ

มากกว่าการใช้อาวุธ


---

# Current Condition


Physical:
บาดเจ็บจากเหตุการณ์ระเบิดและการตกทะเล


Mental:

เหนื่อยล้าจากชีวิตที่ผ่านมา

แต่เป็นครั้งแรกที่เขาไม่มีคำสั่งจากใคร


Goal:

เอาตัวรอด

ค้นหาสถานที่อยู่

และเริ่มต้นชีวิตในแบบที่ตัวเองเลือก


---

# Starting Point

Episode Start:

โจเซ่ตื่นขึ้นมาบนชายหาดของเกาะที่ไม่รู้จักใน East Blue

พร้อมกับคำถามเดียว:


"จากนี้ไป...
ชีวิตของฉันจะเป็นของฉันเองได้หรือยัง?"

---

# Character Summary


## Jose Whiteman


Role:
Future Ship Doctor


Age:
18


Origin:
East Blue


Devil Fruit:
Tori Tori no Mi, Model: Phoenix


Combat Style:
Defensive Counter Fighter


Specialization:
Medical + Rokushiki + Haki


---

# Character Identity

โจเซ่ ไวท์แมน คืออดีตเด็กที่ถูกฝึกให้เป็นอาวุธ
แต่เลือกเดินเส้นทางของหมอ


เขามีพลังของฟีนิกซ์
ความสามารถระดับตำนาน

แต่สิ่งที่เขาต้องการมากที่สุดไม่ใช่พลัง


เขาต้องการ:

อิสระ


---

# Battle Identity

ไม่ใช่นักสู้สายบุก

รูปแบบหลัก:

รับ → วิเคราะห์ → สวนกลับ


ใช้:

- Observation Haki
- Armament Haki
- Rokushiki
- Phoenix Regeneration


เพื่อควบคุมสนามรบ


---

# Medical Identity

โจเซ่เป็นหมอที่:

- ไม่ชอบงาน
- ชอบบ่น
- ดูเหมือนไม่จริงจัง


แต่เมื่อมีชีวิตคนอยู่ตรงหน้า

เขาจะทิ้งทุกอย่างและทำหน้าที่


---

# Narrative Hooks

เรื่องราวสำคัญของโจเซ่:

1. ความจริงเกี่ยวกับพ่อ
2. เหตุผลที่ถูกมอบผลฟีนิกซ์
3. อดีตการฝึกกับ CP
4. การค้นหาความหมายของคำว่าอิสระ
5. การสร้างครอบครัวใหม่บนเรือ


---

# Current Story Status

Beginning of Adventure:

โจเซ่ ไวท์แมน

อดีตแพทย์ทหารเรือ
ผู้รอดชีวิตจากเหตุการณ์ทำลายฐานทัพ

กำลังเริ่มต้นชีวิตใหม่

ไม่มีชื่อเสียง
ไม่มีพรรคพวก
ไม่มีเป้าหมายที่ยิ่งใหญ่กว่าอิสระ

มีเพียงความฝันเดียว:

เป็นหมอที่ช่วยผู้คนทั่วโลก
ในแบบที่ตัวเองเลือก

# =====================================================
# FILE : 02_PLAYER\CHARACTER_CREATION.md
# =====================================================

# CHARACTER CREATION SYSTEM

---

# Purpose

The Character Creation System defines the standardized process for creating a new playable character.

Character creation establishes a character's identity, history, motivations, and starting capabilities while ensuring compatibility with the Narrative Simulation Engine.

Every player character begins as an ordinary individual living within the world, not as a legendary figure.

---

# Design Philosophy

Character creation prioritizes narrative identity over mechanical optimization.

A compelling character is defined by:

- Personality
- Goals
- Relationships
- Strengths
- Weaknesses
- Life Experience

Statistics exist to support storytelling, not replace it.

---

# Character Creation Timing

New characters are created during:

- Session 0
- Character Replacement
- Approved Story Introduction

Characters are never generated during active scenes unless required by the Game Master.

---

# Character Creation Workflow

Every character follows the same creation sequence.

1. Character Concept

↓

2. Basic Identity

↓

3. Background

↓

4. Occupation

↓

5. Attributes

↓

6. Skills

↓

7. Traits

↓

8. Flaws

↓

9. Dream

↓

10. Ambition

↓

11. Relationships

↓

12. Starting Equipment

↓

13. Starting Reputation

↓

14. Final Review

↓

Character Approved

---

# Character Concept

The player first defines a simple concept.

Examples:

- Honest Marine Recruit
- Traveling Doctor
- Former Shipwright
- Treasure Hunter
- Wandering Musician
- Island Hunter
- Merchant Bodyguard

The concept guides all later decisions.

---

# Basic Identity

Every character records:

- Name
- Age
- Gender
- Height
- Appearance
- Birth Island
- Native Sea
- Dominant Hand
- Languages
- Distinguishing Features

Identity provides narrative context only unless otherwise specified.

---

# Background

Background represents the character's early life before gameplay.

Background influences:

- Knowledge
- Starting Relationships
- Initial Opportunities
- Narrative Hooks

Background does not guarantee future success.

---

# Occupation

Occupation represents the character's profession before joining the story.

Examples include:

- Fisher
- Cook
- Carpenter
- Sailor
- Doctor
- Hunter
- Merchant
- Guard

Occupation grants appropriate starting experience and skills.

---

# Attributes

Core Attributes define the character's natural capabilities.

Attributes are assigned according to the Attribute System.

No attribute may exceed the starting limits defined by the engine.

---

# Skills

Skills represent learned experience.

Skills originate from:

- Background
- Occupation
- Training
- Life Experience

Skill assignment follows the Skill System.

---

# Traits

Traits describe positive personal qualities.

Examples:

- Calm
- Brave
- Curious
- Patient
- Observant
- Charismatic

Traits influence narrative interpretation and specific mechanics where applicable.

---

# Flaws

Every character must possess meaningful flaws.

Examples:

- Reckless
- Proud
- Impatient
- Gullible
- Cowardly
- Stubborn

Flaws create dramatic opportunities and meaningful decisions.

---

# Dream

Every playable character possesses one major dream.

Examples:

- Discover a legendary island
- Cure every known disease
- Build the strongest ship
- Become the greatest navigator
- Protect a homeland

Dreams provide long-term motivation.

Dreams may evolve through play.

---

# Ambition

Ambition defines the character's immediate direction.

Unlike dreams, ambitions are achievable within shorter timeframes.

Examples:

- Earn enough money for a ship
- Join a famous crew
- Become a Marine Officer
- Learn Haki
- Find a skilled mentor

Ambitions change throughout the campaign.

---

# Relationships

Characters begin with meaningful social connections.

Relationships may include:

- Family
- Friends
- Rivals
- Teachers
- Employers
- Former Crewmates

Relationships exist independently within the world.

---

# Starting Equipment

Characters receive starting equipment based on:

- Occupation
- Background
- Story Circumstances

Equipment is intentionally modest.

Legendary equipment is never available during character creation.

---

# Starting Reputation

New characters begin with an initial reputation determined by their history.

Most characters begin as unknown individuals.

Exceptional backgrounds may grant minor recognition at the Game Master's discretion.

---

# Character Validation

Before approval, every character must satisfy all engine requirements.

Validation confirms:

- Complete Information
- Legal Attribute Allocation
- Legal Skill Allocation
- Valid Background
- Valid Occupation
- Required Trait
- Required Flaw
- Dream Defined
- Ambition Defined

Incomplete characters cannot enter play.

---

# Character Approval

The Game Master performs the final review.

Approval ensures:

- Narrative Consistency
- Mechanical Balance
- World Compatibility

Approved characters officially become part of the living world.

---

# Replacement Characters

Replacement characters follow the same creation process.

Existing world events remain unchanged.

Replacement characters enter the story naturally through narrative circumstances.

---

# Engine Principles

The Character Creation System follows:

- Story Before System
- Narrative Simulation
- Hidden But Fair
- World Before Player
- No Plot Armor

Character creation establishes the beginning of a life within the world, not the beginning of the world itself.

# =====================================================
# FILE : 02_PLAYER\CHARACTER_GROWTH.md
# =====================================================

# CHARACTER GROWTH SYSTEM

---

# Purpose

The Character Growth System defines how a character develops throughout the Narrative Simulation Engine.

Growth is not measured solely by increased capability.

It also represents changes in personality, knowledge, relationships, reputation, responsibilities, and worldview.

A stronger character is not always a more mature character.

---

# Design Philosophy

Characters grow because they live.

Every challenge leaves an impact.

Every victory teaches confidence.

Every failure teaches humility.

Every relationship changes perspective.

Growth reflects experience rather than numerical progression.

---

# Growth Philosophy

Character Growth represents long-term personal development.

Growth should feel:

- Earned
- Believable
- Meaningful
- Gradual
- Permanent

There are no instant transformations.

---

# Sources of Growth

Characters grow through:

- Adventure
- Failure
- Success
- Training
- Leadership
- Responsibility
- Friendship
- Rivalry
- Loss
- Discovery
- Sacrifice
- Survival

Meaningful experiences create meaningful growth.

---

# Areas of Growth

Growth may occur in many different areas.

Examples include:

Physical Growth

Mental Growth

Emotional Growth

Professional Growth

Social Growth

Leadership Growth

Moral Growth

Philosophical Growth

A character rarely grows in every area equally.

---

# Physical Growth

Physical Growth reflects long-term changes resulting from training and experience.

Examples:

Improved endurance.

Greater strength.

Better coordination.
